project.name: ReaderParser
project.description: ReaderParser is a personal-use Android app that reads webnovels and manhwa from multiple sites through pluggable source implementations. It combines a Jetpack Compose UI, Ktor-based networking, local persistence, background refresh, and dedicated readers for novel and manhwa content. Note: this project has over 100 source files; consider scoping analysis to a subdirectory for faster results.

layers.name: Documentation & Workflow
layers.description: Human-facing docs, operational notes, codemaps, plans, and OpenCode workflow guidance.

layers.name: Build, Tooling & CI
layers.description: Gradle build setup, automation scripts, CI workflows, and repository tooling configuration.

layers.name: Test & QA Assets
layers.description: Unit tests, instrumentation tests, journey specs, and parser fixtures used to verify behavior.

layers.name: Android Resources
layers.description: Android manifest, XML resources, and packaged application assets consumed at runtime.

layers.name: Bootstrap & DI
layers.description: Application bootstrap, Android entry points, and Hilt object-graph wiring.

layers.name: Domain & Core Utilities
layers.description: Android-free domain contracts, immutable models, and shared core helper utilities.

layers.name: Data & Persistence
layers.description: Repository implementations plus Room, filesystem, and DataStore persistence components.

layers.name: Source Plugins
layers.description: Source contracts, registry wiring, and site-specific scraping implementations.

layers.name: UI & Presentation
layers.description: Compose screens, stateless content, navigation, theming, and ViewModel-driven presentation state.

layers.name: Background Work
layers.description: WorkManager jobs that execute downloads and library refresh flows outside the UI lifecycle.

tour.title: Project Overview
tour.description: Start with the human-facing documents to understand the app’s purpose, its plugin-based reading model, and the repository’s architecture rules before opening code.

tour.title: Build and Android Bootstrap
tour.description: These files show how the single Android module is assembled and how the application process starts with Hilt and the main activity.
tour.languageLesson: Kotlin-based Gradle scripts and Android entry points keep build logic and app startup in the same language family, which makes the project easier to navigate end to end.

tour.title: Navigation and UI Shell
tour.description: The Compose shell lives here: navigation routes, destination wiring, and the Material theme that wraps the app’s screens.

tour.title: Domain Contracts and Invariants
tour.description: Read the pure domain layer next. It defines the Android-free repository contracts and the immutable models that every other layer depends on.
tour.languageLesson: The domain layer is intentionally Android-free Kotlin. Data classes and sealed interfaces make invariants explicit and easy to test on the JVM.

tour.title: Source Plugin Boundary
tour.description: These files define the stable scraping boundary. The app talks to Source contracts and a registry, while concrete site plugins stay isolated in their own packages.

tour.title: Repositories and Persistence
tour.description: This is the orchestration layer. Repository implementations bridge source plugins to Room, filesystem storage, and DataStore-backed preferences.

tour.title: Screen Presentation Pattern
tour.description: The app repeats a four-file screen pattern: Screen, Content, ViewModel, and UiState. Study one feature set to learn how presentation is organized across the app.

tour.title: Separate Novel and Manhwa Readers
tour.description: ReaderParser intentionally keeps its two reading experiences separate. These files show how text-based novel reading and image-based manhwa reading diverge in UI and state handling.

tour.title: Background Downloads and Refresh
tour.description: Background work reuses the same repository graph outside the UI lifecycle. Follow these workers to understand download execution and library refresh behavior.

tour.title: Verification and Automation
tour.description: Finish with the project’s quality gates: tests, parser fixtures, and GitHub Actions workflows that keep the app buildable and the sources stable over time.
